one_liner: A 42-year-old man presents to the ED at 2 AM with an excruciating stabbing pain behind his left eye, tearing, and nasal congestion that has recurred nightly for the past 10 days.

stage2.hpi_extension: He describes the pain as **'a hot poker being driven through my left eye'** — constant, boring, and so severe that he paces around the room and bangs his fist against the wall. Attacks start abruptly, **peak within minutes, last about 60-90 minutes, then resolve completely**. For the past 10 days he has had **1-2 attacks every night, always at approximately the same time**. During the attack his **left eye tears, the left eyelid droops, the left pupil appears small, and his left nostril runs**. He has had similar 'bouts' in the spring of each of the last two years, lasting 4-6 weeks, then complete remission. **Alcohol** reliably triggers an attack during a bout.
stage2.social_history: Construction foreman. **Heavy smoker (1 pack/day for 20 years)**. Drinks beer on weekends, but has avoided alcohol since the current bout started.
stage2.physical_exam: Restless, pacing, clutching the left side of his head and rocking. Left eye: **conjunctival injection, profuse lacrimation, partial ptosis, and a miotic pupil (ipsilateral partial Horner syndrome)**. Left nostril with rhinorrhea. Forehead sweating on the left side. Cranial nerves otherwise intact, no focal motor or sensory deficit, no meningismus, no papilledema.

lab_results.result: MRI brain with contrast including dedicated pituitary imaging: **normal**. No mass lesion, no cavernous sinus abnormality, no vascular lesion. Pituitary gland normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Imaging is obtained in patients with trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias at least once to exclude secondary causes (pituitary tumor, cavernous sinus lesion, arterial dissection). Normal imaging supports a primary cluster headache diagnosis.

lab_results.result: Sodium 139, potassium 4.0, glucose 102, creatinine 0.9, liver enzymes within normal limits.

stage5.final_clue: He is placed on **high-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 12 L/min** and given **subcutaneous sumatriptan**. The attack aborts within 10 minutes. **Verapamil** is started for prevention of further attacks during the current cluster period, and a brief prednisone taper is added as a bridge.

debrief.classic_presentation: Cluster headache classically presents in **middle-aged men (male predominance ~3-4:1)** as an **excruciating, strictly unilateral periorbital or temporal pain** lasting 15 minutes to 3 hours. Attacks occur 1-8 times per day, **often waking the patient at the same time each night**, and cluster together over weeks to months (the 'cluster period'), followed by long remissions. Unlike migraineurs, patients are **restless and agitated** (pacing, rocking) rather than wanting to lie still. Autonomic signs are ipsilateral and prominent: **lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea/nasal congestion, ptosis, miosis (partial Horner syndrome), facial sweating, and eyelid edema**. **Alcohol** is a common trigger during a bout.
debrief.pathophysiology: Cluster headache is a **trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia**. Activation of the **trigeminal-autonomic reflex** (trigeminal afferents synapsing with parasympathetic efferents via the superior salivatory nucleus and sphenopalatine ganglion) produces the parasympathetic autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea). The circadian rhythmicity and seasonal periodicity implicate the **posterior hypothalamus**, which shows increased activity on PET and structural change on voxel-based MRI during attacks. Partial sympathetic dysfunction (along the internal carotid plexus) produces ipsilateral ptosis and miosis (Horner-like findings).
debrief.treatment_overview: Acute abortive therapy: **100% oxygen at 12-15 L/min by non-rebreather mask** (first-line) and **subcutaneous or intranasal sumatriptan**. Transitional (bridging) therapy: **prednisone taper** or suboccipital steroid injection until preventive therapy takes effect. Preventive therapy: **verapamil (first-line)**; alternatives include lithium, topiramate, and galcanezumab (anti-CGRP). Avoid alcohol and nitroglycerin during cluster periods.
high_yield_pearls: Cluster headache = unilateral periorbital pain in a restless, agitated middle-aged man with ipsilateral lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, and miosis (partial Horner syndrome).
Attacks cluster at the same time each day/night over weeks and are often triggered by alcohol — think hypothalamic/circadian pacemaker.
First-line acute therapy is 100% oxygen plus subcutaneous sumatriptan.
Verapamil is the first-line preventive drug; steroid taper serves as a bridge.
Cluster headache (and SUNCT) are trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias — contrast with migraine where patients seek dark, quiet stillness.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Neurology and Special Senses chapter, 'Headaches — Cluster headache' section.
